question: What is the primary purpose of the HTML <main> element?
options: To represent the dominant content of the document body
To create a sidebar navigation menu
explanation: The <main> element represents the dominant content of the <body> of a document, directly related to the central topic or functionality.

question: How many visible <main> elements can a single HTML document contain?
explanation: A document must not contain more than one visible <main> element. Multiple instances are only allowed if the extras have the hidden attribute.

question: Which of the following elements must NOT contain a <main> element?
explanation: The <main> element must not be a descendant of <article>, <aside>, <footer>, <header>, or <nav>.

question: What is the implicit ARIA role of the <main> element?
explanation: The <main> element implicitly has a role of 'main' in the accessibility tree.

question: When would it be valid to have two <main> elements in a document?
options: When they are in different sections
When one has the hidden attribute
explanation: Multiple <main> elements are valid only when the additional instances are hidden using the hidden attribute.

question: Where should the <main> element typically be placed in the document structure?
explanation: The <main> element is typically placed directly after the <header> and before the <footer> in the document body.

question: What is a skip-navigation link used for?
options: To skip to the next page
To bypass repetitive navigation and jump to main content
explanation: A skip-navigation link targets the <main> element, allowing keyboard and screen reader users to bypass repetitive navigation links.

question: Which attribute should be added to <main> in single-page applications to improve screen reader announcements?
explanation: Adding aria-live='polite' to <main> helps screen readers announce dynamic content changes in SPAs.

question: What is the default display value of the <main> element?
explanation: The <main> element is a block-level element by default.

question: Should you add role='main' to a <main> element?
options: Only if you have multiple <main> elements
explanation: The <main> element already has an implicit role of 'main', so adding role='main' is unnecessary in modern browsers.

question: What happens when a screen reader encounters a <main> element?
options: It announces 'main landmark' or 'main content start'
It reads it as a regular div
explanation: Screen readers typically announce 'main landmark' or 'main content start' when they encounter the <main> element.

question: Which of these is a valid use of the hidden attribute with multiple <main> elements?
explanation: Both A and C are valid — as long as only one <main> is visible at a time, the others must have the hidden attribute.

question: Which of the following is NOT an allowed ancestor for the <main> element?
explanation: The <main> element must not be inside <header>, <nav>, <footer>, <article>, or <aside>. It can be inside <div> or <body>.

question: What tabindex value should a skip-link target typically have?
explanation: tabindex='-1' allows the element to be programmatically focused (via JavaScript or anchor links) but removes it from the natural tab order.

question: What is the purpose of aria-atomic='true' on a dynamic <main>?
options: To make the element atomic size
To announce the entire region content when any part changes
explanation: aria-atomic='true' tells screen readers to announce the entire region content when any part of it changes, not just the changed portion.

question: Which browsers support the <main> element?
options: All modern browsers including IE with shiv
explanation: All modern browsers support <main>. IE 9-11 have partial support and require an HTML5 shiv.

question: What content should go inside the <main> element?
explanation: The <main> element should contain only the primary content of the page. Navigation, sidebars, and copyright info belong in other semantic elements.

question: Which element should NOT be a child of <main>?
explanation: While <aside> can technically be inside <main>, it's semantically more appropriate to place sidebars outside <main>.

question: What CSS pseudo-class can style the skip link when focused?
explanation: The :focus pseudo-class is used to style elements when they receive keyboard focus, commonly used for skip links.

question: What landmark role do screen readers assign to the <main> element?
explanation: The <main> element maps to the 'main' landmark role, which helps screen reader users navigate to the primary content.
